2: metanoeite (metanoeo) — repent, change one's mind and direction — the verb combines meta (after, change) + noeo (to think, perceive). True repentance is not mere remorse (metamelomai) but a reorientation of the whole person's direction. hengiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near — engiken is perfect tense (has come near and stands near), indicating the kingdom's arrival is not merely future but has approached to proximity. Basileia ton ouranon is Matthew's distinctive phrase (vs. Mark/Luke's basileia tou theou); the ouranon (heavens plural) is the reverential Jewish circumlocution for God.
11: baptizo (baptizo) — to immerse, dip, plunge — used for dyeing cloth, sinking ships, and ritual washings; in Jewish contexts (mikveh) it denoted purification immersion. John's baptism is eis metanoian (unto/for repentance) — the preposition eis indicates purpose or result. He contrasts it with Jesus's baptism en pneumati hagio kai pyri — in/with the Holy Spirit and fire — the preposition en is instrumental: the Spirit and fire are the medium, not the destination.
17: houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapetos — this is my beloved Son — the demonstrative houtos identifies the specific individual; agapetos (beloved, uniquely loved) appears in the LXX at Gen 22:2 for Isaac (the only son, yahid); the term carries the Aqedah resonance of the uniquely beloved son offered. en ho eudokesa — in whom I am well pleased — the aorist eudokesa (to be well pleased, to take delight in) combines the royal Ps 2:7 investiture with the Servant Isa 42:1 anointing; king and servant in one declaration.
1: En de tais hemerais ekeinais — in those days — a temporal bridge after the summary of Jesus's childhood in Nazareth (2:23). The phrase is vague about the intervening period (Jesus's childhood and youth are unnarrated). Iōannes ho baptistēs — John the baptizer — the active participle used as a title (baptistēs), denoting one who regularly baptizes. Keryssōn en te eremo tes Ioudaias — proclaiming in the wilderness of Judea — keryssōn (herald-proclaiming, present participle) indicates ongoing public proclamation; the wilderness of Judea is the Jordan River valley, which immediately fulfills the Isaiah 40:3 oracle cited in v.3.
3: Phone boontos en te eremo — a voice crying in the wilderness — the Isaiah 40:3 citation follows the LXX (which differs from the MT in punctuation: the MT places the wilderness with "prepare the way," but the LXX and Matthew place it with the crying: a voice crying in the wilderness). This punctuation locates John's wilderness ministry as the fulfillment-marker. Hetoimasate ten hodon kyriou — prepare the way of the Lord — kyrios in the LXX citation translates the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) in Isaiah; Matthew applies it to John's preparation for Jesus, implying Jesus is the YHWH whose way is being prepared.
4: trichōmatos kamēlou — camel's hair — the same description as Elijah in 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX (anēr dasys = a hairy man, zōnēn dermatinēn = leather belt). Matthew's readers would recognize the Elijah-correspondence (confirmed in 11:14; 17:12-13). Akridas kai meli agrion — locusts and wild honey — locusts were a permitted food under Levitical law (Lev 11:22); wild honey from rock or tree was a wilderness food (Deut 32:13; 1 Sam 14:25-26). Both are subsistence foods that locate John outside civilized society as a prophetic figure living on creation's provision.
5: Exeporeueto pros auton Ierosolyma kai pasa he Ioudaia kai pasa he perichoros tou Iordanou — Jerusalem was going out to him, and all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan — the imperfect exeporeueto (was going out, kept going out) indicates an ongoing, sustained movement of people over an extended period. The triple pasa (all) — all Jerusalem, all Judea, all the Jordan region — emphasizes the national scope and intensity of John's revival. The geographic compression (center of power + entire province + the border region) signals a movement of whole-population dimensions.
6: Ebaptizonto en to Iordane potamo hyp' autou — they were being baptized by him in the Jordan River — the imperfect passive ebaptizonto indicates ongoing, continuous action: crowds were being baptized one after another over an extended period. Exomologoumenoi tas hamartias auton — confessing their sins — the present participle exomologoumenoi (openly confessing, acknowledging publicly) indicates that the verbal confession accompanied the immersion, making baptism a public act of acknowledged sinfulness. Exomologeo in the middle voice (making a confession on behalf of oneself) carries the sense of voluntary, frank acknowledgment before God and witnesses.
7: Gennēmata echidnōn — offspring of vipers — the vocative combines genealogical metaphor (gennēmata = offspring, products of generation) with viper-imagery. Echidna (viper, adder) was considered particularly dangerous; the offspring inherit the parent's character and venom. Tis hypedexen hymin phygein apo tēs mellousēs orgēs — who warned you to flee from the coming wrath? — the aorist hypedexen (showed, pointed out beforehand) implies someone tipped them off. The question is rhetorically sarcastic: the Pharisees and Sadducees have come to secure the baptismal credential without genuine repentance.
8: Karpous axious tēs metanoias — fruit worthy of repentance — the plural karpous (fruits) suggests multiple visible evidences; a singular fruit would suggest one definitive act. Axios (worthy, proportional, of appropriate weight) measures the fruit against the repentance: genuine repentance produces correspondingly genuine behavioral evidence. The metaphor of fruit-bearing is used throughout Matthew for the test of authentic profession (7:16-20; 12:33; 21:43) — trees are known by their fruit, not by their claims.
9: Patrikas echomen ton Abraam — we have Abraham as our father — the claim of Abrahamic descent as covenantal security was a firmly held conviction in Second Temple Judaism: descent from Abraham placed one within the covenant and its protections. Ek tōn lithōn toutōn dunatos estin ho theos egeirai tekna tō Abraam — from these stones God is able to raise up children for Abraham — the wordplay between lithōn (stones) and tekna (children) may reflect an Aramaic pun: bnaya (children) vs. abnaya (stones). Egeirai (to raise up) is also the resurrection verb — God who raises from the dead can raise children from stones.
10: Ēdē de hē axinē pros tēn rizan tōn dendrōn keitai — already the axe is lying at the root of the trees — ēdē (already, now) makes this present urgency rather than future threat: the axe is not being raised; it is already positioned. The present participle keitai (lies, is placed) confirms its current location. Pan dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai — every tree not producing good fruit is cut down — the present ekkoptetai functions either as a gnomic present (describing the invariable rule) or as an anticipatory present of imminent action: the cutting down is as certain as if it is already happening.
12: Ptyon en tē cheiri autou — the winnowing fork is in his hand — ptyon is the wooden fork used to toss threshed grain into the wind so the lighter chaff separates from the heavier wheat. The present tense (estin implied) makes this present reality, not future promise. Diakathariein tēn alōna autou — to thoroughly clean his threshing floor — diakathariō (compound verb with dia = through + kathariō = cleanse) indicates complete, thorough cleaning. Achyron katakausei pyri asbesto — the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire — asbestos (inextinguishable, quenchless) appears in the LXX for inescapable divine judgment (Isa 34:10; 66:24; Jer 7:20).
13: Tote paraginetai ho Iēsous apo tēs Galilaias epi ton Iordanēn pros ton Iōannēn — then Jesus arrives from Galilee to the Jordan to John — the historical present paraginetai (arrives, comes alongside) brings the narrative into vivid immediacy. This is Jesus's first appearance in Matthew since his childhood in Nazareth (2:23); Matthew gives no account of the intervening years. The tote (then) connects John's ministry as the preparation and Jesus's arrival as the fulfillment of that preparation — what the Baptist announced, the one he announced now arrives.
14: Diekolyen auton — he was trying to prevent/deter him — the imperfect diekolyen suggests persistent, repeated attempts to hinder rather than a single objection. Egō chreian echō hypo sou baptisthēnai — I have need to be baptized by you — the emphatic pronoun egō (I) contrasts with the implied "you"; the construction hypo sou (by you) identifies Jesus as the one who should be baptizing John, not the reverse. The need (chreian) John acknowledges is his own spiritual need for the Spirit-baptism that only Jesus can give (v.11).
15: Prepon hēmin estin plērōsai pasan dikaiosynēn — it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness — prepon (fitting, proper, becoming) is the language of theological and moral appropriateness. Pasan dikaiosynēn — all righteousness — the scope is comprehensive: this one act fulfills the entirety of what God's salvific plan requires. Jesus's baptism is not a confession of sin (he has none) but a covenant-identification with God's people, a commissioning act, and the inauguration of the mission. The plural hēmin (for us) includes both Jesus and John in the propriety of the act.
16: Anebē apo tou hydatos — he came up from the water — apo (from away from) with the water indicates prior immersion: he came up out of it. Ēneōchthēsan autō hoi ouranoi — the heavens were opened to him — anoigō in the aorist passive (ēneōchthēsan) echoes Ezek 1:1 LXX (ēnoichthēsan hoi ouranoi = the heavens were opened) at the inauguration of Ezekiel's prophetic ministry. Peristeron — a dove — in Jewish tradition (b. Chagigah 15a) the Spirit at creation hovered like a dove over the waters (Gen 1:2); the Spirit's dove-form at Jesus's baptism signals a new-creation inauguration.
